mechanism_narrative: Rising industrial production (INDPRO) signals expanding output capacity and solid economic momentum, which elevates corporate earnings expectations and drives S&P 500 returns higher. The relationship holds across all phases of the business cycle without requiring a specific trigger.
failure_condition: Rolling 3-year correlation between monthly INDPRO growth and subsequent 20-day SPX return is negative or below 0.05, or the signal's walk-forward IC is indistinguishable from zero (p-value > 0.10).

mechanism_narrative: A rise in the unemployment rate (UNRATE) above 4% signals a weakening labor market, which reduces consumer spending and corporate profits, leading to negative S&P 500 returns over the following 20 trading days. The signal activates only after the threshold is crossed.
failure_condition: Walk-forward analysis shows that after UNRATE crosses 4%, the 20-day SPX return is positive more than half the time, or the average return is not significantly negative (one-sided t-test p > 0.05).

mechanism_narrative: A simultaneous decline in housing starts (HOUST) and a high federal funds rate (FEDFUNDS above 3%) indicates that tight monetary policy is severely contracting the housing sector, a key economic driver. This joint stress propagates to lower aggregate demand and depresses S&P 500 returns over a quarterly horizon.
candidate_trigger: FEDFUNDS above 3% and HOUST declining over 3 consecutive months
regime_hint: monetary tightening cycle with housing sensitivity
failure_condition: When both conditions are met, the 60-day SPX return is positive in more than 40% of cases or the average return is not significantly negative (p > 0.05 in a one-sample t-test).

mechanism_narrative: During late-cycle expansions characterized by above-trend GDP growth, further increases in nonfarm payrolls (PAYEMS) exacerbate labor shortages and wage inflation, forcing the Federal Reserve to tighten policy earlier than expected, which weighs on equity valuations. The mechanism is active only when the economy is in a late-cycle regime.
failure_condition: In periods where GDP trend growth exceeds 3%, 20-day SPX returns following PAYEMS increases are positive more than 50% of the time, or the negative average return is not statistically significant (p > 0.10).

mechanism_narrative: Strong retail sales (RSAFS) directly reflect robust consumer demand, which boosts corporate revenue and near-term earnings expectations, leading to positive S&P 500 returns within one week. This signal is always active and does not require any conditioning event.
failure_condition: Rolling 1-year correlation between weekly RSAFS surprises and subsequent 5-day SPX returns is negative or below 0.1, or the IC over a 2-year walk-forward is not significantly positive (p > 0.10).

mechanism_narrative: Sustained acceleration in consumer price inflation (CPIAUCSL monthly growth exceeding 0.3% for three consecutive months) erodes real purchasing power and forces the Federal Reserve to raise interest rates, compressing equity valuations. The signal is activated only after this persistent inflationary pattern is observed.
candidate_trigger: CPIAUCSL monthly growth exceeds 0.3% for 3 consecutive months
failure_condition: Following the trigger, 60-day SPX returns are positive more than 50% of the time, or the average return is not significantly negative (one-sided t-test p > 0.05).

mechanism_narrative: When industrial production (INDPRO) rises rapidly (above 1% month-over-month) while consumer price inflation (CPIAUCSL) remains contained (below 3% year-over-year), it signals a non-inflationary expansion that boosts corporate profits and stock prices. The joint condition of strong supply growth with stable prices is necessary for the positive return signal.
candidate_trigger: INDPRO growing >1% month-over-month and CPIAUCSL year-over-year <3%
failure_condition: When both conditions are met, 20-day SPX returns are negative in more than 50% of instances, or the positive average return is not statistically distinguishable from zero (p > 0.10).

mechanism_narrative: In a low interest rate environment (FEDFUNDS ≤ 2%), an increase in housing starts (HOUST) signals strong demand for housing and broader economic confidence, which translates into higher equity returns. The mechanism is conditional on the monetary policy regime; the same rise in HOUST would have a different (negative) effect if rates were elevated.
failure_condition: In months where FEDFUNDS ≤ 2%, the correlation between HOUST growth and subsequent 20-day SPX returns is negative or indistinguishable from zero (p > 0.10), or the signal fails at a 95% confidence level in a rolling test.
